\NewDocumentCommand{\langref}{m g}{\textbf{#1\IfNoValueF{#2}{#2}}}
\NewDocumentCommand{\langfam}{m m g}{\textbf{#1$_{#2}$\IfNoValueF{#3}{#3}}}
\newcommand{\thislang}{\mathcal{L}}
\newcommand{\poly}{polynomial}
\newcommand{\nopolyunknownquasi}{not polynomial, quasipolynomial unknown}
\newcommand{\nopolyquasi}{not polynomial, quasipolynomial}
\newcommand{\unknownpolyquasi}{polynomial unknown, quasipolynomial}
\newcommand{\unknownboth}{unknown}
\newcommand{\noquasi}{not quasipolynomial}
\newcommand{\CO}{CO}
\newcommand{\VA}{VA}
\newcommand{\CE}{CE}
\newcommand{\IM}{IM}
\newcommand{\EQ}{EQ}
\newcommand{\SE}{SE}
\newcommand{\CT}{CT}
\newcommand{\ME}{ME}
\newcommand{\CD}{CD}
\newcommand{\FO}{FO}
\newcommand{\SFO}{SFO}
\newcommand{\NOTC}{$\neg$C}
\newcommand{\ANDC}{$\wedge$C}
\newcommand{\ANDBC}{$\wedge$BC}
\newcommand{\ORC}{$\vee$C}
\newcommand{\ORBC}{$\vee$BC}
\newcommand{\compilespoly}[2]{#1 compiles to #2 with polynomial blowup}
\newcommand{\compilesquasi}[2]{#1 compiles to #2 with quasipolynomial blowup}
\newcommand{\nocompilespoly}[2]{#1 does not compile to #2 with polynomial blowup}
\newcommand{\nocompilesquasi}[2]{#1 does not compile to #2 with quasipolynomial blowup}
\newcommand{\supportspoly}[2]{#1 supports #2 in polynomial time}
\newcommand{\supportsquasi}[2]{#1 supports #2 in quasipolynomial time}
\newcommand{\nosupportspoly}[2]{#2 is not supported by #1 in polynomial time}
\newcommand{\nosupportsquasi}[2]{#2 is not supported by #1 in quasipolynomial time}
\newcommand{\isin}[1]{#1}
\newcommand{\shortname}[1]{\textbf{Short name:} #1\par}
\newcommand{\fullname}[1]{\textbf{Full name:} #1\par}
\newcommand{\source}[1]{\textbf{Source:} #1\par}
\newcommand{\target}[1]{\textbf{Target:} #1\par}
\newcommand{\language}[1]{\textbf{Language:} #1\par}
\newcommand{\operation}[1]{\textbf{Operation:} #1\par}
\newcommand{\status}[1]{\textbf{Status:} #1\par}
\newcommand{\selector}[1]{\textbf{Selector:} #1\par}
\newcommand{\assuming}[1]{\textbf{Assuming:} #1\par}
\newcommand{\titlefield}[1]{\textbf{Title:} #1\par}
\let\titlemacro\title
\renewcommand{\title}[1]{\titlefield{#1}}
\newenvironment{description}{\par\noindent\ignorespaces}{\par}
\newenvironment{language}{}{}
\newenvironment{concept}{}{}

\titlemacro{Tractable Circuit Zoo: Language Definitions}
\date{\today}
\begin{document}
\maketitle

\begin{language}
\shortname{\langref{CNF}}
\fullname{Conjunctive Normal Form}
\begin{description}
A \langref{CNF} is a conjunction of clauses $\bigwedge_{i=1}^mC_i$. A clause is a disjunction of literals $C_i=\bigvee_{j=1}^{k_i}l_j$ where a literal $l_j$ is a variable $x$ or its negation $\lnot x$. For example, $(x \lor y \lor \lnot z)\land (x\lor \lnot y)\land (z\lor w)$.
\end{description}
\end{language}

\begin{language}
\shortname{\langref{cSDD}}
\fullname{Compressed Sentential Decision Diagrams}
\begin{description}
\langref{cSDD} $= \bigcup_T \langfam{cSDD}{T}$. \citep{VanDenBroeck_2015,Darwiche_2011}
\end{description}
\end{language}

\begin{language}
\shortname{\langref{d-DNNF}}
\fullname{Deterministic Decomposable Negation Normal Form}
\begin{description}
Boolean circuits with AND and OR gates; literals as inputs; and whose AND gates are \emph{decomposable} (children mention disjoint sets of variables) and whose OR gates are \emph{deterministic} (on any input, at most one child evaluates to $1$).\citep{Darwiche_2001b}
\end{description}
\end{language}

\begin{language}
\shortname{\langref{d-SDNNF}}
\fullname{Deterministic Structured Decomposable Negation Normal Form}
\begin{description}
\langref{d-SDNNF} $= \bigcup_T$ \langfam{d-SDNNF}{T}. \citep{Pipatsrisawat_2008}
\end{description}
\end{language}

\begin{language}
\shortname{\langfam{d-SDNNF}{T}}
\fullname{Deterministic Structured Decomposable Negation Normal Form (wrt a fixed vtree)}
\begin{description}
For each fixed vtree $T$, a \langfam{d-SDNNF}{T} is a \langref{d-DNNF} that is additionally structured decomposable, respecting the vtree $T$.

Intuitively, all AND gates over the same set of variables must decompose via the same partition, as specified by the vtree.

Equivalently, \langfam{d-SDNNF}{T} formulas are \langfam{SDNNF}{T} formulas that are also \emph{deterministic} (see \langref{d-DNNF}).\citep{Pipatsrisawat_2008}
\end{description}
\end{language}

\begin{language}
\shortname{\langref{DNF}}
\fullname{Disjunctive Normal Form}
\begin{description}
A \langref{DNF} is a disjunction of terms $\bigvee_{i=1}^mT_i$. A term is a conjunction of literals $T_i=\bigwedge_{j=1}^{k_i}l_j$ where a literal $l_j$ is a variable $x$ or its negation $\lnot x$. For example, $(x \land y \land \lnot z)\lor (x\land \lnot y)\lor (z\land w)$.

Equivalently, a \langref{DNF} is a \langref{DNNF} of depth two with an output OR gate whose children are AND gates over literals.
\end{description}
\end{language}

\begin{language}
\shortname{\langref{DNNF}}
\fullname{Decomposable Negation Normal Form}
\begin{description}
Boolean circuits with AND and OR gates, literals as inputs, and whose AND gates are \emph{decomposable} (children mention disjoint sets of variables).\citep{Darwiche_2001a} Decomposability may be seen as the analogue in Boolean circuits to the notion of \emph{syntactic multilinearity} in arithmetic circuits.\citep{shpilka2010arithmetic}
\end{description}
\end{language}

\begin{language}
\shortname{\langref{FBDD}}
\fullname{Free Binary Decision Diagram}
\begin{description}
Binary decision diagrams such that each root-to-sink path tests each variable at most once. Also known as read-once branching programs.\citep{Wegener_2000}
\end{description}
\end{language}

\begin{language}
\shortname{\langref{IP}}
\fullname{Prime Implicants}
\begin{description}
A \langref{DNF} that is the disjunction of all minimal entailing terms (prime implicants). \citep{Quine_1952}
\end{description}
\end{language}

\begin{language}
\shortname{\langref{MODS}}
\fullname{Models}
\begin{description}
The set of satisfying assignments (models) of the function.\citep{Darwiche_2002}

Equivalently, a suitably restricted \langref{DNF} whose terms are in one-to-one correspondence with the models.
\end{description}
\end{language}

\begin{language}
\shortname{\langref{nFBDD}}
\fullname{Nondeterministic Free Binary Decision Diagram}
\begin{description}
An \langref{nFBDD} is a \emph{nondeterministic} \langref{FBDD}, i.e., an \langref{FBDD} that is additionally allowed to contain \emph{guess} nodes.\citep{Wegener_2000}

Also known as a nondeterministic read-once branching program.
\end{description}
\end{language}

\begin{language}
\shortname{\langref{NNF}}
\fullname{Negation Normal Form}
\begin{description}
A Boolean circuit with AND and OR gates with literals as inputs (i.e., negations may only appear at leaves). Also called \emph{De Morgan circuits}. \citep{Darwiche_2002}
\end{description}
\end{language}

\begin{language}
\shortname{\langref{nOBDD}}
\fullname{Nondeterministic Ordered Binary Decision Diagram}
\begin{description}
\langref{nOBDD} $= \bigcup_{<}$ \langfam{nOBDD}{<}. \citep{Wegener_2000}
\end{description}
\end{language}

\begin{language}
\shortname{\langfam{OBDD}{<}}
\fullname{Ordered Binary Decision Diagram (wrt a fixed variable order)}
\begin{description}
For each fixed variable order $<$, binary decision diagrams such that each root-to-sink path tests each variable at most once and the variables appear in the order $<$.\citep{Bryant_1986}

Equivalently, oblivious read-once branching programs respecting the variable order $<$.\citep{Wegener_2000}

Equivalently, \langfam{OBDD}{<}{s} are \langref{FBDD}{s} required to respect the order $<$ on every root-to-sink path.
\end{description}
\end{language}

\begin{language}
\shortname{\langref{PI}}
\fullname{Prime Implicates}
\begin{description}
The subset of \langref{CNF} where formulas are the conjunction of all minimal entailed clauses (prime implicates). \citep{Quine_1952}.
\end{description}
\end{language}

\begin{language}
\shortname{\langref{SDD}}
\fullname{Sentential Decision Diagram}
\begin{description}
\langref{SDD} $= \bigcup_T$ \langfam{SDD}{T}. \citep{Darwiche_2011}
\end{description}
\end{language}

\begin{language}
\shortname{\langfam{SDD}{T}}
\fullname{Sentential Decision Diagram (wrt a fixed vtree)}
\begin{description}
\langfam{SDD}{T} generalizes \langfam{OBDD}{<} by allowing decisions, not only on variables, but on representations (i.e., recursively defined \langref{SDD} formulas).\citep{Darwiche_2011}

Equivalently, an \langfam{SDD}{T} is a \langfam{d-SDNNF}{T} in which determinism is replaced by \emph{strong determinism}, where $\lor$-gates must take the form $ f(x,y) = \bigvee_{i=1}^{m}(p_i(x) \land s_i(y))$, where the $p_i$ are mutually exclusive and exhaustive.
\end{description}
\end{language}

\begin{language}
\shortname{\langref{SDNNF}}
\fullname{Structured Decomposable Negation Normal Form}
\begin{description}
\langref{SDNNF} $= \bigcup_T$ \langfam{SDNNF}{T}. \citep{Pipatsrisawat_2008}
\end{description}
\end{language}

\begin{language}
\shortname{\langfam{SDNNF}{T}}
\fullname{Structured Decomposable Negation Normal Form (wrt a fixed vtree)}
\begin{description}
For each fixed vtree $T$, an \langfam{SDNNF}{T} is a \langref{DNNF} that is additionally structured decomposable, respecting the vtree $T$.\citep{Pipatsrisawat_2008}

Intuitively, all AND gates over the same set of variables must decompose via the same partition, as specified by the vtree.
\end{description}
\end{language}

\begin{language}
\shortname{\langref{uFBDD}}
\fullname{Unambiguous Free Binary Decision Diagram}
\begin{description}
The \emph{unambiguous} variant of \langref{nFBDD}.\citep{Wegener_2000} That is, on any input there is at most one accepting sequence of guesses.
\end{description}
\end{language}

\begin{language}
\shortname{\langref{uOBDD}}
\fullname{Unambiguous Ordered Binary Decision Diagram}
\begin{description}
The \emph{unambiguous} variant of \langref{nOBDD}.\citep{Wegener_2000} That is, on any input there is at most one accepting sequence of guesses.
\end{description}
\end{language}

\begin{language}
\shortname{\langref{OBDD}}
\fullname{Ordered Binary Decision Diagram}
\begin{description}
Binary decision diagrams such that each root-to-sink path tests each variable at most once and the variables appear in the same order.\citep{Bryant_1986}

Also known as oblivious read-once branching programs.\citep{Wegener_2000}

Equivalently, \langref{OBDD} $=\bigcup_{<}$ \langfam{OBDD}{<}.\citep{Darwiche_2002}
\end{description}
\end{language}

\begin{language}
\shortname{\langfam{TDD}{T}}
\fullname{Tree Decision Diagram (wrt a fixed vtree)}
\begin{description}
For each fixed vtree $T$, a \langfam{TDD}{T} is a deterministic tree decision diagram respecting $T$, as introduced by \citet{Capelli_2026}.
\end{description}
\end{language}

\begin{language}
\shortname{\langref{TDD}}
\fullname{Tree Decision Diagram}
\begin{description}
\langref{TDD} $= \bigcup_T$ \langfam{TDD}{T}. \citep{Capelli_2026}
\end{description}
\end{language}

\begin{language}
\shortname{\langref{dec-DNNF}}
\fullname{Decision Decomposable Negation Normal Form}
\begin{description}
Boolean circuits with AND and OR gates; literals as inputs; and whose AND gates are \emph{decomposable} (children mention disjoint sets of variables) and whose OR gates must appear in the form of \emph{decisions/tests} (subcircuits of the form $(x\land f)\lor (\bar{x}\land g)$ where $x$ is a variable).\citep{darwiche2007language}

Equivalently, \langref{dec-DNNF} formulas are \langref{d-DNNF} formulas whose OR-gates may only appear in \emph{decisions/tests}.

Equivalently, \langref{dec-DNNF} formulas are (up to syntax) \langref{FBDD}{s} that may additionally contain decomposable AND-gates.
\end{description}
\end{language}

\begin{language}
\shortname{\langref{dec-SDNNF}}
\fullname{Decision Structured Decomposable Negation Normal Form}
\begin{description}
\langref{dec-SDNNF} $= \bigcup_T$ \langfam{dec-SDNNF}{T}. \citep{Amarilli_2018}
\end{description}
\end{language}

\begin{language}
\shortname{\langfam{dec-SDNNF}{T}}
\fullname{Decision Structured Decomposable Negation Normal Form (wrt a fixed vtree)}
\begin{description}
For each fixed vtree $T$, \langfam{dec-SDNNF}{T} is the subset of \langref{dec-DNNF} that respects $T$.\citep{darwiche2007language,Amarilli_2018}

Equivalently, \langfam{dec-SDNNF}{T} formulas are \langfam{SDNNF}{T} formulas whose OR-gates may only appear in \emph{decisions/tests}.
\end{description}
\end{language}

\begin{language}
\shortname{\langfam{cSDD}{T}}
\fullname{Compressed Sentential Decision Diagrams (wrt a fixed vtree)}
\begin{description}
\langfam{cSDD}{T} is the subset of \langfam{SDD}{T} that is \emph{compressed}, a restriction that makes the representation canonical (i.e., there is a unique representation for every function). \citep{Darwiche_2011}
\end{description}
\end{language}

\begin{language}
\shortname{\langfam{nOBDD}{<}}
\fullname{Nondeterministic Ordered Binary Decision Diagram (wrt a fixed variable order)}
\begin{description}
An \langfam{nOBDD}{<} is a \emph{nondeterministic} \langfam{OBDD}{<}, i.e., an \langfam{OBDD}{<} that is additionally allowed to contain \emph{guess} nodes.\citep{Wegener_2000}

Also known as a nondeterministic oblivious read-once branching program respecting variable order $<$.
\end{description}
\end{language}

\begin{language}
\shortname{\langfam{uOBDD}{<}}
\fullname{Unambiguous Ordered Binary Decision Diagram (wrt a fixed variable order)}
\begin{description}
The \emph{unambiguous} variant of \langfam{nOBDD}{<}.\citep{Wegener_2000} That is, on any input there is at most one accepting sequence of guesses.
\end{description}
\end{language}
\bibliographystyle{plainnat}
\bibliography{refs}
